issues.original: The README claims five minutes, but I'd say ten. The extra five accounts for the common Node version mistake.
issues.fixed: The README claims five minutes, but I'd say ten. The extra time accounts for the common Node version mistake.

issues.fixed: The project is strict about this. Older Node versions will install, but the gateway will throw errors with optional chaining in some places.

issues.original: DashScope is the easiest option if you're in mainland China. Latency to their endpoints from any domestic network is under 200ms. No proxy, no VPN, no fuss.
issues.fixed: DashScope is the easiest option if you're in mainland China. Latency to their endpoints from any domestic network is under 200ms. No proxy, no VPN, no fuss.

issues.original: If you are in mainland China: DashScope endpoints are domestic and fast. Anthropic and OpenAI endpoints require either a proxy, a Hong Kong VPS forwarding requests, or a SOCKS5 tunnel. OpenClaw respects [CODE] and [CODE] environment variables, so if you already have a proxy running locally you can export those before starting the gateway. Do not waste time debugging 'connection timeout' errors before checking whether you can actually reach the provider's endpoint from your network.
issues.fixed: If you're in mainland China: DashScope endpoints are domestic and fast. Anthropic and OpenAI endpoints require a proxy, a Hong Kong VPS, or a SOCKS5 tunnel. OpenClaw respects [CODE] and [CODE] environment variables, so if you already have a proxy running, export those before starting the gateway. Don't waste time debugging 'connection timeout' errors before checking if you can reach the provider's endpoint from your network.

issues.original: Five things that trip people up, in order of frequency:
issues.fixed: Here are the five most common issues, in order of frequency:

issues.original: Run the onboarding wizard. It writes a config file into [CODE]:
issues.fixed: Run the onboarding wizard. It writes a config file into [CODE]:

issues.original: You should see something like: The gateway is a long-running process. In the next pieces we attach channels and skills to it. For now it is sitting there with no input.
issues.fixed: You should see something like: The gateway is a long-running process. In the next steps, we'll attach channels and skills to it. For now, it's idle with no input.

issues.original: Open a second terminal and run: You get a chat-like terminal UI. Try a few things in order:
issues.fixed: Open a second terminal and run: You get a chat-like terminal UI. Try these steps in order:

issues.original: This triggers the [CODE] tool. In the gateway log you will see a line like: That confirms the agent made an outbound HTTP request, got a response, and is now summarizing it.
issues.fixed: This triggers the [CODE] tool. In the gateway log, you'll see a line like: This confirms the agent made an outbound HTTP request, received a response, and is now summarizing it.

issues.original: You can use OpenClaw for months without reading this. But the first time you need to write a skill, debug a misrouted message, or figure out why the agent forgot something, you'll want to know what each component does.
issues.fixed: You can use OpenClaw for months without reading this. But the first time you need to write a skill, debug a misrouted message, or figure out why the agent forgot something, you'll want to know what each component does.

issues.original: I will walk top to bottom.
issues.fixed: I'll walk through it from top to bottom.

issues.original: A Channel is the code that turns 'a DingTalk Stream message' into 'a normalized OpenClaw message' and vice versa. Each channel has its own quirks: DingTalk sends Stream events over a WebSocket, Telegram polls or webhooks, Discord uses a Gateway WebSocket of its own. The Channel layer hides all of that.
issues.fixed: A Channel is the code that converts 'a DingTalk Stream message' into 'a normalized OpenClaw message' and vice versa. Each channel has its own quirks: DingTalk sends Stream events over a WebSocket, Telegram uses polling or webhooks, and Discord uses a Gateway WebSocket. The Channel layer abstracts these differences.

issues.original: What you need to remember: - Channels are configured per-instance. You can run with zero, one, or twenty. - A message goes Channel → Gateway → Agent → Gateway → Channel. The Channel doesn't talk to the Agent directly. - Per-channel rate limits and quirks live in the channel adapter — that's why DingTalk replies feel different from Telegram replies.
issues.fixed: Key points: - Channels are configured per instance. You can run with zero, one, or twenty. - A message flows from Channel → Gateway → Agent → Gateway → Channel. The Channel doesn't communicate directly with the Agent. - Per-channel rate limits and quirks are handled by the channel adapter, which is why DingTalk replies differ from Telegram replies.

issues.original: The most common failure is WebSocket disconnection that isn't auto-recovered. DingTalk Stream connections drop after 300 seconds of inactivity; if the channel adapter doesn't detect this and reconnect, messages pile up in the broker and you see delivery delays ranging from seconds to never. Check [CODE] for 'channel reconnect' events and the timestamp gap. If you see gaps over 5 minutes, your keep-alive isn't working.
issues.fixed: The most common issue is WebSocket disconnection that isn't automatically recovered. DingTalk Stream connections drop after 300 seconds of inactivity. If the channel adapter doesn't detect this and reconnect, messages pile up in the broker, causing delivery delays from seconds to never. Check [CODE] for 'channel reconnect' events and the timestamp gap. If you see gaps over 5 minutes, your keep-alive isn't working.

issues.original: The Gateway runs on [CODE]. It accepts messages from any channel, deduplicates them (DingTalk sometimes redelivers), assigns or restores a session, and hands the message to the Router. The Gateway is also the only thing that talks to the model provider. Every tool result, every memory read, every prompt assembly goes through it. That's why you only need one set of API keys.
issues.fixed: The Gateway runs on [CODE]. It accepts messages from any channel, deduplicates them (DingTalk sometimes redelivers), assigns or restores a session, and passes the message to the Router. The Gateway is also the only component that communicates with the model provider. All tool results, memory reads, and prompt assemblies go through it, so you only need one set of API keys.

issues.original: If ten users send messages simultaneously, the Gateway serializes LLM calls but doesn't throttle ingress. You will see HTTP 429 from the provider, which the Gateway retries with exponential backoff (max 3 retries). If all three fail, the user gets 'I'm having trouble thinking right now' and the turn is logged to [CODE]. The fix is either upgrading your provider tier or configuring [CODE] in [CODE] to match your quota.
issues.fixed: If ten users send messages simultaneously, the Gateway serializes LLM calls but doesn't throttle ingress. You'll see HTTP 429 from the provider, and the Gateway retries with exponential backoff (up to 3 retries). If all retries fail, the user gets 'I'm having trouble thinking right now,' and the turn is logged to [CODE]. To fix this, upgrade your provider tier or configure [CODE] in [CODE] to match your quota.

issues.original: The Router decides *which* agent should handle a message — relevant only if you've configured multiple agents (the default install has one, called Pi). Sessions are how OpenClaw keeps a conversation in WeChat distinct from a conversation in Telegram even though they go to the same agent. Session ID is [CODE].
issues.fixed: The Router decides which agent should handle a message — relevant only if you've configured multiple agents (the default install has one, called Pi). Sessions help OpenClaw keep conversations in WeChat distinct from those in Telegram, even if they go to the same agent. The Session ID is [CODE].

issues.original: If you have ever seen 'the agent confused two of my conversations', that is a session-ID collision and almost always a custom-channel bug.
issues.fixed: If you've ever seen 'the agent confused two of my conversations,' that's a session-ID collision, usually caused by a custom-channel bug.

issues.original: The interesting choices OpenClaw makes here: - **Skills are loaded lazily.** Only the manifest is in the system prompt. The body is paged in when the model triggers a skill. This keeps token cost low. - **Tool errors are returned to the model, not raised.** The model gets a chance to recover. This sounds obvious but a lot of agent frameworks just throw. - **The loop has a hard turn-limit.** Default 30. If the agent is still looping at turn 30 it stops and emits a 'I think I'm stuck' message rather than burning your token budget overnight.
issues.fixed: Interesting choices OpenClaw makes: - **Skills are loaded lazily.** Only the manifest is in the system prompt. The body is paged in when the model triggers a skill, keeping token costs low. - **Tool errors are returned to the model, not raised.** This gives the model a chance to recover. While this may sound obvious, many agent frameworks simply throw errors. - **The loop has a hard turn limit.** The default is 30. If the agent is still looping at turn 30, it stops and emits an 'I think I'm stuck' message to avoid burning your token budget overnight.

issues.original: The classic case is [CODE] returning zero results: the model sees 'success: []', decides it needs to refine the query, calls [CODE] again with a slightly different query, gets another empty list, repeats until turn 30. The loop protection catches this, but you burn 30 LLM calls for nothing. The fix is making tools return semantic errors — 'success: false, reason: no results for query' — so the model can give up earlier. You can also lower [CODE] in the agent config if you notice this pattern.
issues.fixed: The classic case is [CODE] returning zero results. The model sees 'success: []', decides it needs to refine the query, calls [CODE] again with a slightly different query, gets another empty list, and repeats until turn 30. The loop protection catches this, but you waste 30 LLM calls. To fix this, make tools return semantic errors like 'success: false, reason: no results for query' so the model can give up earlier. You can also lower [CODE] in the agent config if you notice this pattern.

issues.original: If you only edit one file in OpenClaw, this is it. [CODE] controls models, tools, channels, memory, cron, and skill loading. The onboarding wizard wrote a default; this piece walks the parts you will actually touch.
issues.fixed: If you only edit one file in OpenClaw, this is it. [CODE] controls models, tools, channels, memory, cron, and skill loading. The onboarding wizard created a default; this guide covers the parts you'll actually use.

issues.original: The mistake people make is putting the most expensive model here, 'to be safe.' Don't. The planning loop runs on every turn. Put [CODE] here, and override to [CODE] only inside specific skills that need real reasoning.
issues.fixed: The common mistake is using the most expensive model here, 'to be sure.' Don't. The planning loop runs on every turn. Use [CODE] here and switch to [CODE] only for specific skills that need real reasoning.

issues.original: OpenAI models are useful in two cases: (1) you need GPT-5.4 specifically, or (2) your skill was prototyped with OpenAI and you don't want to rewrite. Otherwise, I avoid them — Claude is better for reasoning, and Qwen is cheaper for bulk work.
issues.fixed: OpenAI models are useful in two cases: (1) you need GPT-5.4 specifically, or (2) your skill was prototyped with OpenAI and you don't want to rewrite. Otherwise, I avoid them—Claude is better for reasoning, and Qwen is cheaper for bulk work.

issues.original: I wouldn't recommend the Coding Plan if you're running OpenClaw on a single laptop for personal use — DashScope's free tier is sufficient. However, I highly recommend it if you're running anything with cron jobs or multiple channels.
issues.fixed: I wouldn't recommend the Coding Plan for personal use on a single laptop—DashScope's free tier is sufficient. However, I highly recommend it for setups with cron jobs or multiple channels.

issues.original: There are 24 other tools, most with sensible defaults. The ones I've edited: - [CODE] — bumped from 50KB to 500KB so the agent can swallow a config file. - [CODE] — restricted to [CODE] and [CODE]. This is the single most useful safety setting.
issues.fixed: There are 24 other tools, most with sensible defaults. The ones I've edited: - [CODE] — increased from 50KB to 500KB so the agent can handle a config file. - [CODE] — restricted to [CODE] and [CODE]. This is the most useful safety setting.

issues.original: The first six pieces got you to a working OpenClaw with a channel and a skill. This one focuses on the part everyone gets wrong on the first install: memory.
issues.fixed: The first six pieces got you to a working OpenClaw with a channel and a skill. This one focuses on the part everyone gets wrong on their first install: memory.

issues.original: Two files matter the most. [CODE] is the index — the agent reads it on every turn, so it has to be short. [CODE] is where the long tail of 'we tried that, it broke because Y' lives. The daily logs are write-heavy and read-rarely; the agent only reaches for them through search.
issues.fixed: Two files matter the most. [CODE] is the index — the agent reads it on every turn, so it must be short. [CODE] is where the long tail of 'we tried that, it broke because Y' lives. The daily logs are write-heavy and read-rarely; the agent only accesses them through search.

issues.original: The mistake I made for two months: dumping everything into [CODE]. By the time it hit 200 lines, every turn was paying ~3k tokens just to load the index, and the agent still couldn't find what it needed. Index ≠ archive.
issues.fixed: The mistake I made for two months: dumping everything into [CODE]. By the time it reached 200 lines, each turn paid ~3k tokens just to load the index, and the agent still couldn't find what it needed. Index ≠ archive.

issues.fixed: Anything that doesn't fit one of these is probably noise. Discard it.

issues.original: The quality of what you store matters more than the quantity. Each memory entry should allow the agent to make a decision without asking you again.
issues.fixed: The quality of what you store matters more than the quantity. Each memory entry should enable the agent to make a decision without asking you again.

issues.original: Now the agent can act without guessing. It knows the provider, the constraint, and the exact behavior rule.
issues.fixed: Now the agent can act without guessing. It knows the provider, the constraint, and the exact behavior rule.

issues.fixed: Here's what a well-structured [CODE] looks like — enough for most solo users:

issues.original: Ten lines. Under 200 tokens to load. The agent knows who you are, what you're working on, where to look, and what not to do. Everything else is in referenced files and gets pulled via search.
issues.fixed: Ten lines. Under 200 tokens to load. The agent knows who you are, what you're working on, where to look, and what not to do. Everything else is in referenced files and gets pulled via search.

issues.original: OpenClaw recognizes five memory types internally. You don't have to use all of them, but understanding the taxonomy helps you route new information correctly.
issues.fixed: OpenClaw recognizes five memory types internally. You don't have to use all of them, but understanding the taxonomy helps you route new information correctly.

issues.original: When in doubt, write it to the daily log. If you find yourself searching for it later, promote it to the right tier.
issues.fixed: When in doubt, write it to the daily log. If you find yourself searching for it later, promote it to the right tier.

issues.original: Long conversations get compacted automatically when the context window gets tight. By default, the agent loses whatever it didn't write down. [CODE] runs a 'save the important stuff' pass *before* compaction:
issues.fixed: Long conversations get compacted automatically when the context window gets tight. By default, the agent loses whatever it didn't write down. [CODE] runs a 'save the important stuff' pass before compaction:

issues.original: Here's a real scenario from before I turned this on. I was 80 turns into a debugging session. Around turn 20, I mentioned switching my embedding provider from OpenAI to SiliconFlow due to rate limits. The agent acknowledged it, and we moved on.
issues.fixed: Here's a real scenario from before I turned this on. I was 80 turns into a debugging session. Around turn 20, I mentioned switching my embedding provider from OpenAI to SiliconFlow due to rate limits. The agent acknowledged it, and we moved on.

issues.original: Around turn 60, context got tight. Compaction trimmed the middle (turns 15-45), removing the provider switch. At turn 70, I asked the agent to re-index. It called OpenAI and failed. Ten minutes wasted on something that should have been permanent knowledge.
issues.fixed: Around turn 60, context got tight. Compaction trimmed the middle (turns 15-45), removing the provider switch. At turn 70, I asked the agent to re-index. It called OpenAI and failed. Ten minutes wasted on something that should have been permanent knowledge.

issues.original: With [CODE] enabled, the flush pass fires first — writing 'Embedding provider changed to SiliconFlow, OpenAI deprecated' into [CODE] before trimming. Post-compaction, the agent reloads and picks it up.
issues.fixed: With [CODE] enabled, the flush pass fires first — writing 'Embedding provider changed to SiliconFlow, OpenAI deprecated' into [CODE] before trimming. Post-compaction, the agent reloads and picks it up.

issues.original: If you only change one default, change this one.
issues.fixed: If you only change one default, change this one.

issues.original: You can run semantic search across the memory files. You need an embedding API. The cheap path is SiliconFlow's [CODE], which is free and good enough:
issues.fixed: You can run semantic search across the memory files. You need an embedding API. The cheap path is SiliconFlow's [CODE], which is free and good enough:

issues.original: You'll only see the benefit after about a week of usage. Before that, there's nothing to search.
issues.fixed: You'll only see the benefit after about a week of usage. Before that, there's nothing to search.

issues.original: Every token spent on memory is a token not spent on conversation. Here's the math.
issues.fixed: Every token spent on memory is a token not spent on conversation. Here's the math.

issues.original: [CODE] at 40 lines costs about 600 tokens — your fixed overhead per turn. Each file chunk from [CODE] costs 200-500 tokens. Default retrieval budget is 2000 tokens, meaning 3-4 chunks per turn.
issues.fixed: [CODE] at 40 lines costs about 600 tokens — your fixed overhead per turn. Each file chunk from [CODE] costs 200-500 tokens. The default retrieval budget is 2000 tokens, meaning 3-4 chunks per turn.

issues.original: Total overhead per turn: 600 + 2000 = 2600 tokens. On a 128k model, that's about 2%. Acceptable.
issues.fixed: Total overhead per turn: 600 + 2000 = 2600 tokens. On a 128k model, that's about 2%. Acceptable.

issues.original: Tune with [CODE]:
issues.fixed: Tune with [CODE]:

issues.original: Why not set it to 4000 or higher? Memory competes with the conversation. At 4000, you're spending 4600 tokens on overhead. In a multi-turn conversation with a 6000-token reply budget, nearly half is used for retrieval. The agent's responses get squeezed.
issues.fixed: Why not set it to 4000 or higher? Memory competes with the conversation. At 4000, you're spending 4600 tokens on overhead. In a multi-turn conversation with a 6000-token reply budget, nearly half is used for retrieval. The agent's responses get squeezed.

issues.original: The sweet spot: 1000 for quick tasks (< 10 turns), 2000 for standard sessions (the default), and 3000 for long research. Never go above 4000 — it crowds out conversation. If the agent misses context at 2000, improve the memory entries, not the budget.
issues.fixed: The sweet spot: 1000 for quick tasks (< 10 turns), 2000 for standard sessions (the default), and 3000 for long research. Never go above 4000 — it crowds out conversation. If the agent misses context at 2000, improve the memory entries, not the budget.

issues.original: Up to v2026.3.6, memory was an explicit tool the agent had to call. If it forgot, it operated without context. From v2026.3.7, memory moved into a hooks lifecycle the harness runs around the agent:
issues.fixed: Up to v2026.3.6, memory was an explicit tool the agent had to call. If it forgot, it operated without context. From v2026.3.7, memory moved into a hooks lifecycle the harness runs around the agent:

issues.original: This chapter is the sequel for everyone in mainland China who needed to ship something past their team's IT department. There are too many channels, the docs are scattered across a dozen READMEs, and most of the 'compare' tables online are out of date.
issues.fixed: This chapter is for everyone in mainland China who needs to get something past their team's IT department. There are too many channels, the docs are scattered across a dozen READMEs, and most 'compare' tables online are outdated.

issues.original: Latency numbers are round-trip message-to-response timings measured from Beijing/Shanghai during business hours. The main cause of high latency is the webhook delivery path — public accounts and WeCom self-built apps route through Tencent's dispatcher, adding 500-1000ms compared to long-poll channels.
issues.fixed: Latency numbers are round-trip message-to-response timings measured from Beijing and Shanghai during business hours. The main cause of high latency is the webhook delivery path. Public accounts and WeCom self-built apps route through Tencent's dispatcher, adding 500-1000ms compared to long-poll channels.

issues.original: Message format support is more important than it seems. Markdown allows your agent to send code blocks and formatted lists. DingTalk and WeCom smart bot handle GitHub-flavored markdown well. WeChat channels require plain text or proprietary card schemas.
issues.fixed: Message format support is crucial. Markdown lets your agent send code blocks and formatted lists. DingTalk and WeCom smart bot handle GitHub-flavored markdown well. WeChat channels require plain text or proprietary card schemas.

issues.original: The local install gets you to 'it works on my machine.' The server install ensures it survives a kernel update.
issues.fixed: The local install gets you to 'it works on my machine.' The server install ensures it survives a kernel update.

issues.original: This chapter walks through the deployment I use on a 2-core 4GB ECS box and the common failures I've documented.
issues.fixed: This chapter walks through the deployment I use on a 2-core 4GB ECS box and the common failures I've documented.

issues.original: Before deploying, choose the right server. Consider these four options:
issues.fixed: Before deploying, choose the right server. Consider these four options:

issues.original: The advantage is proximity to DashScope, reducing API round-trips from 200ms to 20ms when both the gateway and the model are in the same region. The disadvantage is that the Great Firewall can make outbound package installs flaky unless you set up a mirror.
issues.fixed: The advantage is proximity to DashScope, which reduces API round-trips from 200ms to 20ms when both the gateway and the model are in the same region. The disadvantage is that the Great Firewall can make outbound package installs flaky unless you set up a mirror.

issues.original: The dashboard is cleaner, the documentation is better, and package mirrors are unnecessary. The tradeoff is increased latency—150ms per turn—if your model provider is Alibaba or Tencent.
issues.fixed: The dashboard is cleaner, the documentation is better, and package mirrors are unnecessary. The tradeoff is increased latency—150ms per turn—if your model provider is Alibaba or Tencent.

issues.original: The catch is that Hetzner's network is optimized for Europe, so if your users or model endpoints are in Asia, you'll experience higher latency.
issues.fixed: The catch is that Hetzner's network is optimized for Europe, so if your users or model endpoints are in Asia, you'll experience higher latency.

issues.original: Free hardware, full control, and unlimited disk space. The downsides include uptime (your ISP doesn't guarantee five nines), dynamic IPs (you'll need DDNS), and port forwarding (which your router may block for inbound 443).
issues.fixed: Free hardware, full control, and unlimited disk space. The downsides include uptime (your ISP doesn't guarantee five nines), dynamic IPs (you'll need DDNS), and port forwarding (which your router may block for inbound 443).

issues.original: A home server is suitable for prototyping but not for production unless you're the only user.
issues.fixed: A home server is suitable for prototyping but not for production unless you're the only user.

issues.original: Why? The gateway uses 200MB of resident memory, but model responses buffer in RAM before streaming to the client. When a sub-agent forks, the OS temporarily duplicates the parent process. I've seen a 2GB instance run out of memory during a code review task that spawned three sub-agents in parallel. 4GB provides headroom, while 8GB eliminates the problem entirely.
issues.fixed: Why? The gateway uses 200MB of resident memory, but model responses buffer in RAM before streaming to the client. When a sub-agent forks, the OS temporarily duplicates the parent process. I've seen a 2GB instance run out of memory during a code review task that spawned three sub-agents in parallel. 4GB provides headroom, while 8GB eliminates the problem entirely.

issues.original: OS: Ubuntu 22.04. 4GB of RAM is crucial—2GB is sufficient for one agent but becomes insufficient when a sub-agent spawns.
issues.fixed: OS: Ubuntu 22.04. 4GB of RAM is crucial—2GB is sufficient for one agent but becomes insufficient when a sub-agent spawns.

issues.fixed: pm2 supervises the Gateway to prevent crashes from taking you down:

issues.fixed: For the Web Dashboard (port 18789), use nginx with certs from acme.sh:

issues.original: The 600-second read timeout is necessary—long-running agent turns will exceed the nginx default of 60 seconds and fail mid-stream.
issues.fixed: The 600-second read timeout is necessary—long-running agent turns will exceed the nginx default of 60 seconds and fail mid-stream.

issues.original: If you prefer containers, OpenClaw supports Docker. Here is the compose file I use for multi-service deployments:
issues.fixed: If you prefer containers, OpenClaw supports Docker. Here is the compose file I use for multi-service deployments:

issues.original: Three volumes are essential:
issues.fixed: Three volumes are essential:

issues.original: When Docker is better:** You run OpenClaw alongside other services (a database, a vector store, a monitoring stack) and you want one [CODE] to bring everything online. The isolation also makes it easier to test config changes — spin up a second container with a different config, compare behavior, kill the worse one.
issues.fixed: When Docker is better: You run OpenClaw alongside other services (a database, a vector store, a monitoring stack) and want one [CODE] to bring everything online. The isolation also makes it easier to test config changes—spin up a second container with a different config, compare behavior, and kill the worse one.

issues.original: When Docker is worse:** Debugging file permissions — the container runs as root, your host files may not be, and you will spend time with [CODE]. Inspecting logs requires [CODE] or volume mounts. Hot-reloading skills during development is slower because the filesystem sync has a delay. For a single-service deploy where you ssh into the box and tail logs directly, bare-metal is simpler.
issues.fixed: When Docker is worse: Debugging file permissions—the container runs as root, and your host files may not be, so you'll spend time with [CODE]. Inspecting logs requires [CODE] or volume mounts. Hot-reloading skills during development is slower because the filesystem sync has a delay. For a single-service deploy where you SSH into the box and tail logs directly, bare-metal is simpler.
